title: Cookies
tldr: Cookies are small text files (max 4KB) stored by the browser that are sent with every HTTP request to the same domain.
Cookies are managed via the document.cookie API (JavaScript) or Set-Cookie HTTP header (server).
Attributes: domain, path, expires/max-age, secure (HTTPS only), httpOnly (not accessible via JS), sameSite (CSRF protection).
laymanDefinition: Cookies are like sticky notes that a website sticks on your browser. Each time you visit that website, your browser shows all the sticky notes that belong to that site. The notes can say things like 'user is logged in' or 'prefers dark mode'. The server can also stick notes on your browser by sending special headers. Unlike Web Storage (your private filing cabinet), cookies are automatically shown to the server on every visit — that's how websites remember you. But this also means cookies have a tiny size limit (4KB) and affect performance.

deepDive.heading: How Cookies Work
deepDive.text: When a server sends a response, it can include a Set-Cookie header. The browser stores the cookie and includes it in the Cookie header of every subsequent request to the same domain (matching the cookie's domain, path, and other attributes). Cookies are stored as key=value pairs with optional attributes separated by semicolons.

deepDive.heading: Cookie Attributes and Security
deepDive.text: Secure: Only send over HTTPS. HttpOnly: Not accessible via document.cookie — protects against XSS. SameSite: Controls cross-site sending: 'Strict' (same-site only), 'Lax' (top-level navigation allowed), 'None' (any context, requires Secure). Domain/Path: Restricts which URLs receive the cookie. Expires/Max-Age: Sets lifetime. Without expires, the cookie is a session cookie (deleted when browser closes).

deepDive.heading: document.cookie API
deepDive.text: Reading document.cookie returns all non-HttpOnly cookies as a semicolon-separated string of key=value pairs. Writing to document.cookie sets or updates a single cookie: document.cookie = 'key=value; path=/; max-age=3600'. Setting with the same key, path, and domain overwrites the existing cookie. To delete, set max-age=0 or expires to a past date.

deepDive.heading: Cookies vs Web Storage vs IndexedDB
list: Cookies (4KB): Sent with every request. Supports HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite. Best for session tokens and server-required data.
Web Storage (5-10MB): Client-only. Not sent with requests. Best for UI preferences and client-side caching.
IndexedDB (unlimited): Asynchronous, structured data. Best for large datasets (offline apps, file data).
Choose cookies when: The server needs the data on every request, or you need HttpOnly protection.

deepDive.heading: CSRF Protection with SameSite Cookie
deepDive.text: SameSite attribute is the primary defense against Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF). With SameSite=Lax (default in modern browsers), cookies are not sent on cross-site requests initiated by third-party sites (like clicking a malicious link). SameSite=Strict provides maximum protection but may break legitimate cross-site flows. SameSite=None requires Secure and allows all cross-site requests.

interviewAnswer: Cookies are key-value pairs stored in the browser and sent with every HTTP request to the originating domain (max 4KB). They're set via the Set-Cookie HTTP header or via document.cookie in JavaScript. Key attributes: Secure (HTTPS only), HttpOnly (inaccessible to JS — prevents XSS theft), SameSite (CSRF protection), Domain/Path (scoping), and Expires/Max-Age (lifetime). HttpOnly cookies are the most secure way to store authentication tokens since they can't be stolen by XSS attacks. Unlike localStorage, cookies are automatically included in HTTP requests, making them ideal for server-side session management but less suitable for client-only data.

interviewQuestions.question: What are cookies and how do they work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Cookies are small text files (up to 4KB) stored by the browser and sent with every HTTP request to the domain that set them. They are set via Set-Cookie HTTP header or document.cookie in JavaScript.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between a session cookie and a persistent cookie?
interviewQuestions.answer: A session cookie has no Expires or Max-Age attribute and is deleted when the browser closes. A persistent cookie has an expiration date and survives browser restarts until that date.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the HttpOnly flag and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.answer: HttpOnly prevents client-side JavaScript from accessing the cookie via document.cookie. This protects against XSS attacks — even if an attacker injects scripts, they cannot steal HttpOnly cookies. Essential for session/authentication cookies.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the SameSite cookie attribute?
interviewQuestions.answer: SameSite controls when cookies are sent in cross-site requests. SameSite=Lax (default) sends cookies on top-level navigations (clicking a link) but not on embedded requests (images, iframes). SameSite=Strict blocks all cross-site usage. SameSite=None allows all, but requires Secure (HTTPS).

interviewQuestions.question: How do you set a cookie in JavaScript?
interviewQuestions.answer: document.cookie = 'username=Alice; path=/; max-age=86400; secure; samesite=lax'. Each assignment sets or updates one cookie. For multiple cookies, call document.cookie multiple times.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you read all cookies in JavaScript?
interviewQuestions.answer: document.cookie returns all non-HttpOnly cookies as a string: 'key1=val1; key2=val2; key3=val3'. Parse it by splitting on '; ' and then splitting each pair on '='.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you delete a cookie?
interviewQuestions.answer: Set the cookie with the same name, path, and domain but with max-age=0 or expires set to a past date: document.cookie = 'username=; max-age=0; path=/'.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between cookies and localStorage?
interviewQuestions.answer: Cookies (4KB) are sent with every HTTP request and support HttpOnly (not accessible via JS). localStorage (5-10MB) stays client-side only. Use cookies for server-required data (auth tokens); use localStorage for client-only preferences.

interviewQuestions.question: Can an HttpOnly cookie be read via JavaScript?
interviewQuestions.answer: No. HttpOnly cookies are not accessible via document.cookie. They are only sent to the server with HTTP requests. This is a security feature to protect session tokens from XSS attacks.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the Secure flag on a cookie?
interviewQuestions.answer: The Secure flag ensures the cookie is only sent over HTTPS connections. If a page is served over HTTP, a Secure cookie is not sent and cannot be set. This prevents interception of cookies over unencrypted connections.

codeExamples.title: Reading and Writing Cookies with JavaScript
codeExamples.description: Always encodeURIComponent cookie values to handle special characters. Use max-age=0 for deletion. The path must match the original cookie's path.

codeExamples.title: Setting Cookies with All Security Flags
codeExamples.description: Security flags make cookies resilient to common attacks. HttpOnly (server-only) protects against XSS. Secure ensures HTTPS-only transmission. SameSite prevents CSRF.

codeExamples.title: Simple Cookie Utility Class
codeExamples.description: A utility class wraps cookie operations with clean syntax. The RegExp approach for get() handles special characters in cookie names properly.

codeExamples.title: Server-Side Cookie Setting (Node.js/Express)
codeExamples.description: Server-side cookies are set via Set-Cookie headers. HttpOnly flag prevents client-side access — the most secure way to handle auth tokens.

codeExamples.title: Parsing document.cookie String
codeExamples.description: Parsing document.cookie involves splitting by '; ' and '='. Always use decodeURIComponent since cookie values are URL-encoded.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the maximum size of a cookie?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Each cookie is limited to approximately 4 KB (4096 bytes). This includes the name, value, and attributes.

mcqQuestions.question: Which flag prevents JavaScript from accessing a cookie?
mcqQuestions.explanation: HttpOnly makes the cookie inaccessible to document.cookie in JavaScript. Essential for protecting session tokens against XSS.

mcqQuestions.question: What does the Secure flag on a cookie do?
options: Ensures the cookie is only sent over HTTPS
Prevents the cookie from being modified
Makes the cookie accessible only on the server
mcqQuestions.explanation: Secure ensures the cookie is only transmitted over HTTPS connections, preventing interception over unencrypted HTTP.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the purpose of the SameSite attribute?
mcqQuestions.explanation: SameSite controls when cookies are sent in cross-site requests, protecting against Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF).

mcqQuestions.question: How do you delete a cookie in JavaScript?
options: Cookies cannot be deleted via JS
mcqQuestions.explanation: Set the cookie with the same name, path, and domain but with max-age=0 or an expired date.

mcqQuestions.question: What happens when a session cookie (no expires) is set?
options: It's deleted when the browser closes
It's deleted after the page refresh
mcqQuestions.explanation: A session cookie has no Expires or Max-Age attribute and is deleted when the browser session ends (browser closes).

mcqQuestions.question: How are cookies sent to the server?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Cookies matching the request domain/path are automatically included in the Cookie HTTP header with every request.

mcqQuestions.question: Which attribute specifies which URLs should receive a cookie?
mcqQuestions.explanation: The Domain and Path attributes restrict which URLs the cookie is sent to. Domain specifies the host, Path specifies the URL prefix.

mcqQuestions.question: Can JavaScript access HttpOnly cookies?
options: No, they are hidden from JavaScript
Only if the cookie was set via JS
mcqQuestions.explanation: HttpOnly cookies are not accessible via document.cookie. They are only sent to the server with HTTP requests.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the difference between SameSite=Strict and SameSite=Lax?
options: Strict is for HTTPS, Lax is for HTTP
Strict blocks all cross-site usage; Lax allows top-level navigations
mcqQuestions.explanation: SameSite=Strict blocks cookies on all cross-site requests. SameSite=Lax allows cookies on top-level navigations (clicking a link to a different site) but blocks embedded requests (images, iframes, fetch).
